games.explanation: Backing the Dodgers at a near coin-flip price feels almost rude given the names involved, but that's the gift when the market gets distracted by the pitching matchup. Yes, Skenes is the best arm on the planet, but he's also human against left-handed bats, and the Dodgers happen to run out the most dangerous lefty alive at the top of the order. Ohtani's .452 xwOBA versus righties is the kind of number that doesn't care who's on the mound, and Freeman tilts the same direction behind him. Lauer isn't anyone's idea of a stopper, but he doesn't need to be brilliant against a Pittsburgh lineup whose best hitter sits at a .338 xwOBA. At -104, you're getting the better team, the better lineup, and the scarier left-handed bats for a flat price.

Chase Burns is a strikeout machine against righties and a batting practice arm against lefties, which is a problem in a building where San Diego runs Tatis, Merrill, and Machado at him in the same lineup. The split is night and day, with lefties posting a .323 xwOBA and barreling him at better than eleven percent, exactly the profile Merrill and Tatis were built to exploit. Giolito isn't dazzling, but he doesn't need to be against a Reds offense leaning on Bleday and a few small-sample bats. The market is paying for the strikeout reel and ignoring the half of the matchup where Burns gets hit hard. Getting plus money on the better team in a coin-flip game is the whole story.

J.T. Ginn has a tidy stat line that falls apart the second a left-handed bat steps in, and Milwaukee happens to roll out the deepest lefty-heavy order he's seen all year. Yelich and Turang are the exact profiles that have turned Ginn into a batting practice arm against southpaws, where his xwOBA balloons past .350 and the barrel rate nearly doubles. The Brewers are also just the better team by a wide margin here, with an Elo gap that says this should be priced as a clear favorite, not a coin flip. -110 on the superior club drawing the platoon mismatch is the kind of line you take and stop asking questions.
